mappings.rationaleAI: Hormones regulate homeostasis (C.1), the stress alarm reaction via adrenalin and cortisol (C.5), and reproductive processes (A.5). Their role in metabolism, growth, and mood connects to aging (A.6), and hormonal balance is a key criterion for defining normal health (B).

Circadian rhythms govern the internal body clock whose most prominent human manifestation is the sleep-wake cycle (D.3), encompassing how sleep is defined and generated (D.3.a) and the daily psychophysiological variations in physical, behavioural, and mental states (D.3.b).

Humoral medicine defined health as balance among four bodily fluids, establishing a foundational framework for normality in human health (B). Therapeutic regimens of dietary regulation, bloodletting, and purging timed to seasons connect directly to nutrition and therapeutic diets (D.1, D.1.e).

The transition from humoral definitions of health as bodily balance (B) to germ theory by Pasteur and Koch enabled vaccination and public health measures constituting modern defenses against disease (C.2), particularly the immune response (C.2.d).

Penicillin as the first major antibiotic augmented bodily defenses against bacterial infection (C.2), and the episode's treatment of antimicrobial resistance highlights the evolutionary challenge to sustaining immune and pharmacological defenses (C.2.d).

Germ theory, antibiotics, vaccines, and sterilization are foundational to understanding and supporting bodily defenses against disease (C.2) and the immune response (C.2.d). Microbes outnumbering human cells in the body underscores microbiology's direct relevance to health maintenance.

Mitochondrial ATP production is central to maintaining the cellular internal environment and powering vital processes (C.1), and mitochondrial dysfunction is directly linked to aging and neurodegenerative disease (A.6).

Pain perception via nociceptive neural pathways is a key bodily mechanism for detecting and responding to threats during stress (C), and individual variation in pain thresholds reflects the alarm-response system preparing the body for flight or resistance (C.5).
